name: Prompt Injection
description: Manipulating LLM via crafted inputs can cause unintended actions. Direct injections overwrite system prompts, while indirect ones manipulate inputs from external sources.
common_examples: Overriding system prompts with malicious instructions
Injecting commands through external data sources (websites, files)
Chaining prompts to bypass safety filters
Using special tokens or formatting to escape context
prevention_strategies: Privilege control and least privilege for LLM actions
Add human in the loop for privileged operations
Segregate external content from user prompts
Establish trust boundaries between LLM, external sources, and user inputs
Monitor LLM input and output for anomalies
example_attack_scenarios: Attacker provides prompt that bypasses content filters
Malicious website content injected via indirect prompt
Chained prompts exfiltrate sensitive data through API calls

name: Insecure Output Handling
description: Insufficient validation of LLM outputs before passing them downstream can lead to XSS, CSRF, SSRF, privilege escalation, or remote code execution.
common_examples: LLM output used directly in web pages without sanitization (XSS)
LLM-generated SQL queries executed without parameterization
LLM output used in system commands without validation (RCE)
Backend functions called based on LLM output without authorization checks
prevention_strategies: Treat LLM as untrusted user - apply input validation to LLM outputs
Encode output before display to prevent XSS/injection
Use parameterized queries for database operations
Implement least privilege for backend operations triggered by LLM
Validate and sanitize LLM responses before downstream use
example_attack_scenarios: LLM generates JavaScript that executes in user's browser
LLM crafts SQL injection payload that bypasses parameterization
LLM output triggers SSRF by including malicious URLs

name: Training Data Poisoning
description: Manipulation of training data or fine-tuning processes to introduce vulnerabilities, backdoors, or biases that compromise security, effectiveness, or ethical behavior.
common_examples: Malicious data introduced during pre-training or fine-tuning
Poisoned data from scraped internet sources
Attacker gains access to data pipeline and injects backdoors
Model trained on biased or manipulated datasets
prevention_strategies: Verify supply chain and source of training datasets
Validate data sources and maintain signed data provenance
Use ML-BOM (Machine Learning Bill of Materials)
Implement anomaly detection for training data
Test model outputs for biased or malicious responses
Conduct adversarial robustness testing
example_attack_scenarios: Attacker submits fake resumes to training set for HR screening LLM
Model trained on web scrapes includes trojan from compromised site
Fine-tuning on malicious dataset creates subtle backdoor

name: Model Denial of Service
description: Attackers cause resource-intensive operations on LLMs, leading to service degradation or high costs. This includes excessive token generation, recursive context expansion, or high-frequency requests.
common_examples: Submitting queries that generate extremely long responses
Sending large volumes of requests to exhaust rate limits
Crafting inputs that trigger recursive or complex reasoning chains
Variable-length input floods
Continuous input overflow
prevention_strategies: Implement rate limiting per user/IP
Set max token limits for requests and responses
Monitor resource usage and set throttling policies
Implement queue management and load balancing
Use input validation to block resource-intensive patterns
Set timeouts for LLM operations
example_attack_scenarios: Attacker submits prompt causing LLM to generate massive output
Recursive prompt pattern causes exponential token consumption
Distributed requests exhaust model capacity and increase costs

name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
description: LLM applications can be compromised through vulnerable components, services, or datasets. This includes third-party models, pre-trained datasets, plugins, and infrastructure dependencies.
common_examples: Vulnerable third-party model or plugin
Outdated libraries or dependencies with known exploits
Poisoned pre-trained models from untrusted sources
Compromised training datasets
Weak model repositories without integrity checks
prevention_strategies: Maintain ML-BOM (Machine Learning Bill of Materials)
Use only verified and signed models from trusted sources
Implement dependency scanning and vulnerability management
Isolate third-party components with least privilege
Monitor for anomalous behavior in model outputs
Regular security audits of supply chain components
example_attack_scenarios: Compromised plugin leaks sensitive data
Backdoored pre-trained model from public repository
Vulnerable dependency enables remote code execution

name: Sensitive Information Disclosure
description: LLMs may reveal confidential data in responses, including training data, system prompts, or PII. This can occur through memorization, prompt manipulation, or insufficient output filtering.
common_examples: LLM reveals training data containing PII
Attacker extracts system prompts through crafted queries
Model memorization leaks proprietary information
Side-channel attacks extract sensitive parameters
Insufficient sanitization of API responses
prevention_strategies: Sanitize training data to remove PII and sensitive information
Implement output filters to detect and block sensitive data leakage
Use differential privacy techniques
Conduct regular data leakage testing
Apply strict access controls and user authentication
Monitor for anomalous extraction patterns
example_attack_scenarios: Attacker crafts prompts to extract memorized credit card numbers
Model reveals internal system architecture through verbose errors
Prompt injection extracts confidential business logic

name: Insecure Plugin Design
description: LLM plugins with insufficient access control, validation, or authorization can be exploited to execute malicious operations, access unauthorized data, or escalate privileges.
common_examples: Plugins accept free-form text without validation
Insufficient authorization checks before executing privileged operations
Plugins trust LLM outputs without validation
Missing input sanitization allows injection attacks
Overly broad plugin permissions
prevention_strategies: Implement strict input validation for plugin parameters
Enforce least privilege - plugins should have minimal necessary permissions
Require explicit user authorization for sensitive operations
Validate and sanitize all plugin inputs
Use parameterized queries and prepared statements
Implement comprehensive logging and monitoring
example_attack_scenarios: Email plugin allows LLM to send phishing emails without user consent
Database plugin accepts unsanitized LLM queries enabling SQL injection
File access plugin grants unrestricted read/write to filesystem

name: Excessive Agency
description: LLM-based systems with excessive functionality, permissions, or autonomy can perform unintended actions causing damage. This occurs when systems trust LLM outputs without sufficient oversight.
common_examples: LLM granted excessive permissions to modify critical systems
Autonomous agents making decisions without human oversight
Unrestricted access to sensitive APIs or databases
LLM executing high-impact operations based on ambiguous instructions
Chaining of multiple actions without intermediate validation
prevention_strategies: Limit permissions granted to LLM-based systems (least privilege)
Require human approval for high-impact operations
Implement operation whitelisting - allow only necessary actions
Track and log all LLM-initiated operations
Use separate API keys with limited scopes
Implement rate limiting and operation throttling
example_attack_scenarios: LLM misinterprets instruction and deletes production database
Autonomous agent executes unauthorized financial transactions
LLM with API access exfiltrates sensitive data through chained calls

name: Overreliance
description: Systems or users excessively depending on LLMs without oversight can face misinformation, miscommunication, security issues, or legal problems due to incorrect or inappropriate content.
common_examples: Accepting LLM-generated code without security review
Using LLM output for critical decisions without human verification
Trusting LLM-generated legal/medical advice without expert review
Deploying LLM-generated content without fact-checking
Automating high-risk processes based solely on LLM recommendations
prevention_strategies: Implement human review for critical operations
Use LLMs as advisory tools, not decision-makers
Cross-reference LLM outputs with authoritative sources
Clearly communicate LLM limitations to users
Implement confidence scoring and uncertainty quantification
Regular validation of LLM outputs against ground truth
example_attack_scenarios: Company deploys LLM-generated code with security vulnerabilities
Medical system makes incorrect diagnosis due to hallucinated information
Legal advice bot provides incorrect guidance leading to compliance violations

name: Model Theft
description: Unauthorized access to proprietary LLMs through theft, copying, or extraction. This includes physical theft, model extraction via API queries, or exploiting infrastructure vulnerabilities.
common_examples: Model weight extraction through repeated API queries
Insider threat - employee copies model files
Infrastructure compromise grants access to model artifacts
Side-channel attacks extract model parameters
Exploiting model serving endpoints to download weights
prevention_strategies: Implement strong access controls for model repositories
Monitor for anomalous query patterns indicating extraction attempts
Use rate limiting to prevent model probing
Encrypt model files at rest and in transit
Implement model watermarking and fingerprinting
Conduct regular security audits of model infrastructure
Restrict API access to authenticated users with monitoring
example_attack_scenarios: Competitor uses API to query model and build equivalent
Attacker exploits S3 misconfiguration to download model weights
Malicious insider copies proprietary model to external storage
